scenario_title: Add and delete the same RR in one UPDATE
test_rationale: This test targets the underspecified interaction of two operations in a single RFC 2136 UPDATE: adding and deleting the exact same RR. The initial zone contains a stable A RR at x.campus.edu. so the UPDATE can act on a known name without needing complex prerequisites. Because the protocol does not say whether the server must apply the Update Section strictly in wire order, collapse duplicate/conflicting edits, or treat them as a no-op, implementations may differ in whether the final RRset is unchanged, transiently modified, or handled as an error-free acceptance with different internal commit semantics. The follow-up QUERY on the touched name makes the final state observable.

group_1.behavior: Treat the add_rr and delete_rr for the identical RR as sequential operations or otherwise normalize them so the update is accepted and leaves the RRset unchanged or absent depending on internal processing order.
group_1.post_state: x.campus.edu. A may remain present as 192.0.2.10 only, or the transient 192.0.2.20 may not be observable.

group_2.behavior: Collapse the conflicting operations or treat the message as a no-op in a way that removes the added RR before commit, leaving only the preexisting RRset state.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §2 and §3.4.2 define UPDATE as a sequence of RRs in the Update Section and specify add/delete RR encodings, but they do not define semantics when the same owner/type/RDATA appears in both an add_rr and delete_rr within one message. RFC 2181 §5.4.1 discusses RR ordering within an RRset, not intra-message update cancellation or ordering. As a result, compliant servers may process, normalize, or collapse the pair differently, producing different post-update zone contents and potentially different reply handling.
